summary:
Ain Ghazal, located on the outskirts of modern Amman in Jordan, was one of the largest known Neolithic settlements in the world during its occupation from approximately 9200 to 5000 BCE. At its peak around 7250 BCE, the site may have housed up to 3,000 inhabitants spread across roughly 15 hectares, making it substantially larger than the contemporary settlement at [[event:2|Jericho]].

The site is renowned for its extraordinary cache of plaster statues discovered in 1983 — among the oldest large-scale human statuary ever found. These figures, made from lime plaster over reed armatures, depicted human forms with carefully painted features and were deliberately buried in pits, suggesting ritual significance. Ain Ghazal provides critical evidence for the complexity of symbolic and religious life in early Neolithic communities of the southern Levant, alongside evidence for mixed farming, herding, and hunting that characterized [[event:18|Pre-Pottery Neolithic B]] subsistence strategies.
